Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionTechnically Complete (Order)  OrderTECOTechnically completed        Is Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionCompleted (Request)           NotificationNOCONotification completed       Is Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionCompleted (Request)           NotificationAPRFApproval: Refused            Is Not Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionWork Not Performed (Order)    OrderNCMPNot Completed                Is Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionWork Not Performed (Order)    OrderCLSDClosed                       Is Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionClosed (Order)                OrderCLSDClosed                       Is Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionClosed (Order)                OrderNCMPNot Completed                Is Not Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionDeletion Flag (Request)       NotificationDLFLDeletion Flag                Is Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionDeletion Flag (Order)         OrderDLFLDeletion Flag                Is Set          
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionTechnically Complete          Order OperationTECOTechnically completed        Is SetOptional
Proactive Maintenance (YA02)PMSP2CompletionClosed                        Order OperationCLSDClosed                       Is SetOptional

 

If you find this information useful, or have any questions, please feel free to leave a comment.

entry.title: Preventive Maintenance assistance for SAP PM and SAP EAM using IBM watsonx
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's fast-paced production industry, unscheduled downtime and equipment breakdowns can cause major operational interruptions and financial losses. To overcome this, businesses are increasingly resorting to proactive techniques that use cutting-edge technology to efficiently manage their assets. One such method is to integrate AI-driven solutions into current corporate systems.

 This article explores how combining Watsonx.ai with SAP's Plant Maintenance (PM) Module and Asset Management Platform can help create a virtual assistant for preventive maintenance. The technology aims to automate asset monitoring, identify potential breakdowns, and schedule timely interventions, ensuring continuous production while reducing unexpected maintenance costs.

 

Architecture

In this architecture, we have SAP S/4HANA at the core, which integrates SAP Plant Maintenance (PM) and SAP Enterprise Asset Management (EAM). These modules handle the maintenance and asset lifecycle management processes within the system. The data exchange between SAP S/4HANA and the front-end interface happens through SAP OData services, which efficiently transfer data to and from a SAP UI5 application—in this case, a Preventive Maintenance Application.

This SAP UI5 Preventive Maintenance Application is enhanced with a conversational layer powered by Watsonx Assistant, allowing users to interactively query and retrieve real-time details about plant operations and asset conditions. Watsonx Assistant is built upon Watsonx.ai, an AI platform capable of processing both structured and unstructured data to provide insights and recommendations related to the maintenance and management of critical assets.

Additionally, SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) serves as a critical integration hub in this architecture. It connects various SAP services and applications, enabling seamless data flow between systems. SAP BTP also incorporates SAP Gen AI Hub service, which can extend the capabilities of the system by integrating with external AI platforms, such as IBM Cloud, which hosts watsonx.ai.

One of the key features of this architecture is its versatility in data sourcing. While SAP HANA serves as the primary database for SAP applications, watsonx assistant can also interface with non-SAP databases, enabling the system to collect, analyse, and utilize data from a variety of sources. This opens the door for more comprehensive insights by merging data from disparate systems, enhancing the overall predictive maintenance capabilities of the virtual assistant. 

 

 Value Proposition 

  • Comprehensive Maintenance Schedules 
    • Time-based Maintenance: Ensures that equipment is maintained on a regular basis at predetermined intervals, lowering the chance of unexpected malfunctions. These schedules may be automated using Watsonx Assistant, which improves adherence to maintenance timeframes while eliminating manual involvement. 
    • Performance-based maintenance: It helps monitors asset performance parameters and initiates maintenance actions when equipment performance begins to depart from ideal circumstances. Integration with WatsonX Assistant allows for predictive insights and faster response to performance deterioration. 
    • Condition-based repair: Monitors real-time equipment conditions using IoT data, allowing for repair when certain thresholds or abnormalities are discovered. The Watsonx assistant may use this information to automate maintenance triggers and maximise asset uptime.  

  • Seamless IoT Integration for Automated Inspections
    • By interacting with an IoT Hub, Watsonx Assistant automates inspection operations by gathering real-time data from equipment sensors. This eliminates manual inspections, decreases the possibility of human mistake, and assures that equipment always operates within set safety and operational limitations.
    • Automated warnings and suggestions based on IoT data can motivate preventive maintenance choices, hence increasing overall operational efficiency.  

  • Streamlined SAP Workflow Management: 
    • Watsonx Assistant improves the administration of important SAP activities including generating, maintaining, and presenting equipment and functional locations. It streamlines the processing of asset information and maintenance records, minimising administrative bottlenecks. 
    • Simplifies the process of maintaining maintenance task lists, allowing plant managers to easily prioritise, allocate, and carry out maintenance tasks. 
    • Allows for the approval and release of maintenance orders via a conversational interface, which speeds up decision-making and reduces delays caused by human participation. 

  • Boosting Operational Efficiency: 
    • Automating procedures and using intelligent maintenance schedules enhances asset availability, uptime, and lowers operating costs. 
    • Watsonx Assistant's integration with SAP enables seamless communication across teams, enhancing collaboration and real-time decision-making.    

Business Outcome:  

  • Increased Operational Efficiency: With the integration of watsonx assistant into SAP’s preventive maintenance, businesses can automate maintenance scheduling and task execution. This reduces the manual workload, optimizes resource allocation, and streamlines maintenance operations. By ensuring equipment is serviced based on real-time data and performance metrics, operations run smoothly without unnecessary interruptions, boosting overall productivity. 
  • Reduced Downtime Costs: By leveraging real-time insights from IoT sensors and predictive maintenance capabilities, watsonx assistant helps prevent unplanned equipment failures. This proactive approach minimizes unexpected downtime, ensuring continuous production and reducing the significant costs associated with halting operations for emergency repairs.  
  • Extended Equipment Lifespan: Regular, condition-based, and performance-based maintenance prolongs the life of critical machinery. By addressing wear and tear before it escalates into serious damage, businesses can avoid costly replacements or overhauls, maximizing the return on investment in their equipment assets. 
  • Improved Safety and Compliance: Automating inspections and maintenance ensures that equipment consistently operates within safety standards and regulatory requirements. watsonx assistant can help ensure that safety protocols are followed, alerting teams when maintenance is overdue or when equipment is performing outside acceptable parameters, reducing the risk of accidents or compliance violations. 
  • Enhanced Customer Satisfaction: Consistent operational efficiency and reduced downtime lead to fewer disruptions in service or product delivery. watsonx assistant’s role in optimizing maintenance ensures that customers receive reliable, timely products and services, leading to higher customer satisfaction and stronger business relationships.

Business Features 

  • Enhanced and Quick Assistance for Asset Troubleshooting: watsonx assistant provides real-time troubleshooting support for operators and maintenance teams. By leveraging AI-driven insights, it quickly identifies potential asset issues, suggests solutions, and provides step-by-step guidance. This reduces troubleshooting time and helps resolve problems before they escalate, minimizing the impact on production. 
  • Automated Analysis of Inspection and Maintenance Activity with Detailed Report Generation: The assistant automates the analysis of inspection data and maintenance logs, generating comprehensive reports without manual input. This feature ensures that maintenance activities are tracked, and detailed summaries of inspections are created, giving managers clear insights into equipment health, compliance, and performance.   
  • User-Friendly Conversational Flow for Operator and Supervisor Persona: With an intuitive conversational interface, watsonx assistant tailors interactions for different user personas like operators and supervisors. It simplifies complex maintenance tasks, offering guidance in an easy-to-understand language, making the entire process more efficient and accessible for all users involved in equipment management. 
  •  Limitless Data Insights and Exploration Capability: watsonx assistant provides powerful analytics and exploration tools for maintenance data. Users can dive deep into equipment performance, historical maintenance records, and predictive insights, enabling informed decision-making and the discovery of hidden patterns or trends that can enhance preventive maintenance strategies. 
  • Ability to Understand Natural Language Queries and Retrieve Precise Answers: Equipped with natural language processing (NLP), watsonx assistant can understand user queries in everyday language. Whether an operator asks for specific equipment information, upcoming maintenance tasks, or troubleshooting advice, the assistant delivers precise answers instantly, reducing the need to sift through complex data manually.  

Conclusion 
Integrating Watsonx Assistant into preventive maintenance operations introduces an AI-driven approach to asset management. It enhances troubleshooting, automates analysis, and provides comprehensive data, helping businesses maintain operational efficiency and minimize downtime. The conversational interface supports both operators and supervisors, streamlining maintenance processes and integrating with SAP systems. Watsonx Assistant enables teams to make informed decisions by analysing maintenance data using natural language processing and data insights. This integration aims to extend equipment lifespan, improve safety, and enhance compliance within maintenance activities, making it a practical addition to asset management strategies. 

Credits: 
Dhara Bagadia 
Tushar Trivedi

entry.title: Configuring Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) In SAP Asset Performance Management (APM)
entry.content:
Have you ever wondered how to predict the Probability Of Failure (PoF) for a technical object associated with a specific failure mode based on its age?. In this blog, I would like to highlight the essential features of Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) in SAP Asset Performance Management (APM).

Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) is a feature within APM that allows the maintenance team to predict when a technical object could fail and how likely  failure would occur given the age of the technical object. The graph that FCA produces is based on Weibull model, a commonly used data model within the realm of reliability. System uses malfunction notifications to train and score the FCA model and visualize the failure data. On the graph, we can view upper and lower confidence intervals (the range in values of likely failure given the technical object`s age) and Probability Of Failure (PoF).

SAP Asset Performance Management

My Business Use Case:

We run an air compressor with a standby equipment continuously to operate the kiln girth gear lubrication system. As a maintenance engineer, I would like to predict the Probability Of Failure (PoF) using Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) in SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) for one of the air compressors before the planned outage in Jul 2025.

My Technical Object Structure in S/4 HANA Asset Management:

MTTR/MTBR Analysis for the technical object 10001729 in S/4 HANA Asset Management:

Before we dive into Failure Curve Analytics, I would also like to touch base on other alternatives to predict next failure date for the technical object 10001729 using MTBF(Mean Time Between Failure) average values. 

  • I assume failure occurs at regular intervals and the next failure date is simply:

  • I assume failure occurs randomly over time and use random model with exponential distribution to predict next failure date:

 

Now, let us dive into Failure Curve Analytics and see when the Air Compressor will fail if it runs continuously.

First things first, check the integrity of the Air Compressor 10001729 in SAP APM.

To view the actual failure curve graph, we need to create a FCA model. FCA model needs notifications to train and score. For now, all the notifications & failure modes that will be used to train and score have already been created in the S/4 HANA Asset Management system that is associated with the SAP APM system. 

Create a Technical Object Group and assign the respective technical objects which are being used for FCA model.

 

 

Creating Failure Curve Analytics Model:

 

If we have any model configurations, we can see them once we click on "Configure Failure Curve Analytics".

I have created a new FCA model as,

  • The age for conditional probability of failure is used to calculate the conditional probability of the failure. For example, if you enter 100 days as the age and your technical object has a current age of 50 days, then the conditional probability of failure is calculated for the age of 100 days given that the technical object has not failed until the age of 50 days. If you leave blank, then system calculates only the probability of failure.
  • First Installation date is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its first failure. If installation date is not maintained, the first failure date is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its next failure.
  • If technical object is repairable and restored to a "like-new" condition and set to the age of 0 once it is repaired. This age is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its next failure. If not repairable, the installation date is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its failure.

Under Input Data Sets, let us assign the technical objects that are being trained and scored within the FCA model. We can "Add" or "Remove" the technical objects from the model.

Now, let us assign the failure modes. All the failure modes associated with the failure data profile of the technical object used in FCA model are displayed. We can check off the failure modes to be excluded from training and scoring of the model.

Note: It is considered to have no more than 40 failure modes associated with the model.

Now let us specify the malfunction notification date range to train and score the FCA model. A default date range is provided by the system, although this can be altered if desired.

 

Training and Scoring the Model:

With all the input Data Sets properly configured, the FCA model AC_SL200_FCA can now be trained and scored.

If the training and scoring fails, system displays the failure list that prevented the model from completing successful training and scoring and the status will be set to "Failed".

If the training and scoring of the model are both successful, the status of the both will be set to "Completed".

 

If we want to run the model at a different interval, we can still change the interval to DAY, WEEK & MONTH.

 

Viewing the Analytics Graph of the FCA Model:

Now the FCA Model "AC_SL200_FCA" has been successfully trained and scored. The FCA analytical graph can be viewed within the technical object details page. 

Let us navigate to "Explore Technical Objects" tile.

 

on the graph, there are three curves. The main one is probability of failure curve, which is the likely percent chance the equipment 10001729 will fail because of the failure mode "Low Discharge Pressure" selected at a specified age in days. The upper and lower confidence intervals mark the highest and lowest percent chance that the equipment 10001729 will fail due to the "Low Discharge Pressure" failure mode. The predicted failure date is Jan 8, 2025, and the time to failure is  47 days.

Key Note:

The MTBF approach to predict next failure date based on average values can mask variations and specific trends in the failure data. They assume constant failure rate, and might not reflect reality.

The FCA with Weibull distribution is more flexible and can represent various failure modes and can model different failure behaviors over time. When we consider systems and their components, they often do not fail at constant rate, So the FCA analysis can account for varying failure rates over time and fits the actual failure data to a curve and predicts when future failures might occur, where as the MTBF cannot. 

This brings me to the end of my process walk-through. I hope this blog proves useful to Plant Maintenance team. I look forward to seeing Plant Maintenance team using SAP APM Failure Curve Analytics to predict the technical object failure. 

If you would like to keep up on SAP Sustainability Control Tower, Asset Management and Asset Performance Management, follow me on SAP Community and LinkedIn.

 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy.C

My LinkedIn Profile: www.linkedin.com/in/pardhreddyc

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | 2023 FPS02 Release
entry.content:
Welcome back to my blog post series about Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition!

On the 8th October 2024, SAP has released the second “Feature Pack Stack (FPS)”, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition for the 2023 release.
For a comprehensive overview of selected innovation highlights across all lines of business, see the blog post 

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | 2023 FPS02 Release – Part 1

In this blog post we will dive deeper into the innovations for Maintenance Management as part of the line of business Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Release 2023 FPS02. 

 

Table of Contents

Enhancements in area of Phase Model 

  • Skipping screening phase in reactive maintenance process
  • Enhanced configuration for assigning overall status profile
  • Evaluation of maintenance order before entering next subphase

Inspection Checklist Process

  • Inspection Checklist Process Enhancements
  • Integrated checklist-handling in Find Technical Objects and Find Maintenance Orders and Operations
  • New SAP Fiori app Manage Inspection Checklists

Closed Loop

  • Integrated, cross-app navigation from the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app and Find Maintenance Orders app through BAdI implementation
  • Cross-application navigation from maintenance-order processing to permit to work 
  • Recommendations from SAP Asset Performance Management in the SAP Fiori app “Create Maintenance Request”
  • Enhanced integration of SAP Asset Performance Management into task lists and maintenance items for SAP Fiori apps
  • Providing a catalog-based “Failure Effect” field in the SAP Fiori apps for maintenance requests
  • Enabling situation handling for maintenance task lists and maintenance plans 

Enhancements for Perform Maintenance Jobs app

  • Overview of Team assignments
  • Overview of PRT assignments
  • Overview of planned Services
  • Planned Work and Remaining Work
  • Business Catalog for Cross-App Navigation

Purchase Contract Details in Maintenance Orders and Task Lists

Improve accuracy of material costs in refurbishment process

AI-assisted Maintenance Order Recommendation

Mass change of custom fields using the “Manage Maintenance Plans” and “Manage Maintenance Items” apps

Mass Generation of Permit to Work Objects

More Information / Additional Links

 

Enhancements in area of Phase Model 

Configuring Requirements for Creating Phase-Enabled Orders - Skipping screening phase in reactive maintenance process

If you create maintenance requests and maintenance orders of a specific type, these maintenance requests and orders are processed according to phases and become part of a comprehensive end-to-end process. In this business process, a standard logic controls the prerequisites that must be fulfilled before you can create a
phase-enabled maintenance order in the Planning phase. The underlying SAP standard logic is as follows:

  • Maintenance orders of the Reactive Maintenance order type must always be based on an accepted
    maintenance request. The application logic of the order evaluates that the header notification has been accepted during the screening phase, i.e. system status APOK (Approval: OK) has been activated. In other words: You can only create a reactive maintenance order in the Planning phase if the corresponding maintenance request has the system status APOK (Approval: OK) and is in the Accepted (Request) subphase. As soon as the accepted maintenance request has been assigned to a maintenance order, it is renamed to "maintenance notification". This header notification requires a priority and a final due date.
    In the case that the header notification does not include this active system status, the reactive maintenance order will not be created.
  • Maintenance orders of the Proactive Maintenance order type do not necessarily need to be based on a
    maintenance request. They can also be created based on a maintenance plan, for example. If you base a proactive maintenance order on a maintenance request, the maintenance request does not need to have run through an approval process.

With the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Release 2023 FPS02, configuration and a Business Add-In (BAdI) is provided that allows you to overwrite the standard application logic:

The new Business Add-In (BAdI) BAdI: Processing of Phase-Enabled Maintenance Orders
(EAM_CREATE_PHASE_ENABLED_ORDER) and the Customizing activity Define Requirements for Creating
Phase-Enabled Orders (Customizing view V_EAM_NOTIFCNFG) allow you to determine whether maintenance
requests need to be approved before phase-enabled maintenance orders can be based on them.

With the Customizing view V_EAM_NOTIFCNFG and the BAdI method CREATE_PHASE_ENABLED_ORDER, you can determine different combinations of planning plants, notification types and order types for which you want to implement a business logic different from the SAP standard described above. On the level of the maintenance planning plant, you can determine for each combination of notification type and order type whether an accepted maintenance request is mandatory for creating the corresponding phase-enabled maintenance order. For example, you can determine that in a specific planning plant, reactive maintenance orders can be created based on maintenance requests that have not been accepted. Or you can determine that proactive maintenance orders only can be created based on accepted maintenance requests.

In the example below,

  • a reactive maintenance order of type YA01 and maintenance planning plant 1010 can be raised against a notification of type M1. 
  • the notification Type M1 has no Approval Required flag active, so that any notification of that type will not enter the screening phase at all. Hence, those notification will never have system status APOK (Approval: OK) active.
  • without this configuration, the reactive maintenance order could not have been raised against a notification of type M1.

You can access the corresponding activity in Customizing: Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Fiori Apps for Maintenance Processing -> General Settings -> Define Requirements for Creating Phase-Enabled Orders

Figure 1: Customizing - Change View “View for controlling the creation of phase-enabled orders”: Overview

In addition, see Customizing: Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Maintenance and Service Notifications -> Overview of Notification Type

Figure 2: Customizing - Change View “Partner Functions, Approval”: Details

In the case that the configuration does not fulfil your business requirements, you can implement the BAdI EAM_CREATE_PHASE_ENABLED_ORDER. If you have entered the corresponding data in the Customizing view, the BAdI is called in the following cases:

  • You create a phase-enabled maintenance order in the Create Maintenance Order app.
  • You create a phase-enabled maintenance order in the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app.
  • You create a phase-enabled maintenance order via the BAPI BAPI_ALM_ORDER_MAINTAIN. 

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

Enhanced configuration for assigning overall status profile

If you process your maintenance orders according to phases, your business transactions lead to specific system statuses that are set on maintenance order or order operation level. These system statuses determine
the subphases and phases to which the corresponding maintenance order or order operation is passed. The subphases with their conditions and sequence throughout the maintenance process are bundled in an
overall status profile that is assigned to an order type. We deliver a standard configuration for the Reactive
Maintenance and Proactive Maintenance process.

Up to now, you could only specify the overall status profile depending on the order type.

Figure 3: Prior 2023 FPS02 release: Customizing view - Assign Overall Status Profile to Order Type

However, you might need to modify this standard configuration slightly depending on your various
maintenance planning plants. With the 2023 FPS02 release, you can also specify the valid overall status profiles depending on a combination of order type and maintenance planning plant.

Figure 4: Starting with 2023 FPS02 release: Customizing view - Assign Overall Status Profile to Order Type

You can access the corresponding activity in Customizing: Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Fiori Apps for Maintenance Processing -> General Settings -> Configure Overall Status Profiles

Both screenshots shown above have the same consequence, all maintenance orders of type YA01 regardless of their maintenance planning plant will be assigned to the same overall status profile PMSP1.

Note:

  • Following restriction note 3365558, the configuration of the phase model is delivered by SAP and customers are not allowed to modify this configuration.
  • Future enhancements might remove some restrictions, so that customers might have several overall status profiles assigned to different combinations of maintenance planning plant and order types.

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

Evaluation of maintenance order before entering next subphase

A phase-model enabled maintenance order is assigned to an overall status profile which defines the conditions (using system statuses) determining each subphase of the order header and operation.

The new Business Add-In (BAdI) BAdI: Evaluation of Maintenance Order Data Including Buffer
(EAMS_EVAL_ORDER_DETAILS) allows you to evaluate data of phase-enabled maintenance orders each time
the maintenance orders are created or passed to a new subphase. This enables customers to introduce their custom logic to prevent the transition of the maintenance order into the next subphase.

As an example, a maintenance order has been released and the execution of this business transaction would determine a new subphase so that this order would be placed in the preparation phase.
Due to custom logic implemented in that BADI and the actual data of that maintenance order, the execution of this business transaction can be blocked so that this order remains in the current phase/subphase.

The BAdI reads maintenance order data from the buffer before the order data is saved on the database. You can evaluate this maintenance order data and implement warning or error messages that prevent the system from saving the order changes from the buffer to the data base. Note that you cannot use this BAdI to directly change any order data.
With this BAdI, you can evaluate and compare the following maintenance order data:

  • Order header data
  • Order operation data
  • Order operation component data
  • Data of the order object list
  • Phase control codes set on order header and on order operation level

This BAdI is called if you process phase-enabled maintenance orders

  • on the SAP Web User Interface for Plant Maintenance (PM)
  • in SAP Fiori apps
  • via the BAPI BAPI_ALM_ORDER_MAINTAIN
  • via the RAP-based API Maintenance Order (Version 2)

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

Inspection Checklist Process

For using the inspection checklist process

  • the business function LOG_EAM_CHECKLIST must be activated,
  • the checklist functionality must be enabled.

Check out also the Expert Talk: Inspection Checklist in Maintenance Management – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

We launched the inspection checklist solution first with the 2021 release and enhanced it with 2023 FPS01 release. What's new with FPS02?

Inspection Checklist Process Enhancements

In this chapter the description of each enhancement starts with a figure about the Phase Model for the Maintenance Process to provide you the context for phase-enabled orders. 

Maintenance Order: New Form Template for the Output Management

Figure 5: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With this feature, you can enhance a maintenance order by a new output type (CHECKLIST_REPORT for application object type MAINTENANCE_ORDER) and a new form and email template (EAM_MJP_CHECKLIST_REPORT / EAM_MJP_CL_DFLT_TMPL) . This enhancement is supported both by the classic SAP GUI as well as by WebDynpro.

Figure 6: 'Change Maintenance Order' app - new Output Type and Form Template

The new form template is an enhanced version of the old form with the following improvements:

  1. Barcode information is available for the checklist and is printed.
  2. Information on order and selected operations are printed.
  3. Checklist type of the operation is printed.
  4. Control indicator gets printed:
    + Required characteristic
    + Optional characteristic
    + After accepted characteristic
    + After rejected characteristic
    + Calculated characteristic (informational)
  5. Inspection characteristic and control indicator is printed together to avoid single lines
    at the end of a page

Figure 7: Maintenance Order: New Form Template for the Output Management

Note:

  • Deactivated and canceled checklists are not printed.
  • Inspection characteristics with the control indicators Do Not Print and No Charc Recording are not printed.
  • The fom template is translated.

 

Work Pack: New Form Template for Output Management

Figure 8: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With this feature, you can enhance the standard inspection checklist process for Work Pack Creation or Print in the Find Maintenance Order (F2175) and Find Maintenance Order and Operation (F2173) apps. With the new form and email template (PM_ORD_CHECKLIST_REPORT / EAM_MAINT_ORD_CL_DFLT_TMPL), the new output type (CHECKLIST_REPORT for application object type MAINTENANCE_JOB_PACKAGE), and the new grouping criterion (Operation Checklist Type), you can bundle job packs based on the checklist type functionality of order operations.

Figure 9: 'Find Maintenance Order' app - New grouping attribute 'Operation Checklist Type'

Figure 10: Work Pack - new Output Typ, Form Template, and Grouping Attribute

The new form template is an enhanced version of the old form with the following improvements (find the numbering also on the screenshot below):

  1. Barcode information is available for the checklist and is printed.
  2. Work pack/job pack information is printed.
  3. Checklist type of the operation is printed.
  4. Control indicator gets printed:
    + Required characteristic
    + Optional characteristic
    + After accepted characteristic
    + After rejected characteristic
    + Calculated characteristic (informational)
  5. Inspection characteristic and control indicator is printed together to avoid single lines
    at the end of a page

Figure 11: Work Pack / Job Pack - New Form Template for the Output Management

Note:

  • Deactivated and canceled checklists are not printed.
  • Inspection characteristics with the control indicators Do Not Print and No Charc Recording are not printed.
  • The fom template is translated.

 

Perform Maintenance Job app (F5104A)

Figure 12: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With the Perform Maintenance Job app you can now create follow-on notifications for inspection checklists by selecting the new button (see red dotted line in the screenshot below) in the checklists table of a job. The popover that is displayed when you select the status bar in the checklists table now shows additional details, including the usage decision valuation of the relevant inspection lot (see green dotted line). You can also display the usage decision valuation in the results table (see yellow dotted line) to inform the technician, if the inspection lot is still open for results recording.

Figure 13: 'Perform Maintenance Job' app enhancements

The Create Follow-On Notification uses the Notification Type from the Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants activity and the technical object from the inspection lot.  A reference to the notification in the document flow is created.

Figure 14: 'Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants' activity

Note: The Create Follow-On Notification is disabled, if the inspection lot is deactivated or has a recorded usage decision.

 

Final Time Confirmation: New system check of operations with checklists

New customizing flag Check Results Recording Status at Final Confirmation is available in customizing activity Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants.

Figure 15: Customizing activity 'Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants'

When the flag is set then the system checks if results have been recorded for all required or processed inspection characteristics. This check gets bypassed for deactivated, canceled or usage decision valuated inspection lots.

Final time confirmation check failed in IW41 transaction:

Figure 16: Final time confirmation check failed in IW41 transaction

Final time confirmation check failed in Perform Maintenance Jobs app:

Figure 17: Final time confirmation check failed in 'Perform Maintenance Jobs' app

 

Inspection Lot Usage Decision: New Follow-Up Actions

Figure 18: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With the new Follow-Up Actions you can enable the system to automatically change the system status of a technical object during checklist completion. For this purpose, three new function modules are available that you can specify as follow-up actions for usage decisions in Customizing. The function modules either set or reset the new technical object system status FLCO (Faulty Condition).

The following new function modules are available in function group EAM_CL_FOL:

  • EAM_CL_SET_TOBJ_STATUS_FLCO (Checklists Follow-Up Action: Set FLCO status on technical object)
    This follow-up action can be used to set the new technical object status FLCO independently of the inspection lot usage decision valuation:
    - The FLCO status becomes active, if it is not active.
    - You can use this follow-up action for example to accept an inspection lot based on visual characteristics, but the technical object has a technical faulty condition.
  • EAM_CL_RES_TOBJ_STATUS_FLCO (Checklists Follow-Up Action: Reset FLCO status on technical object)
    This follow-up action can be used to reset the new technical object status FLCO independently of the inspection lot usage decision valuation:
    - The FLCO status becomes inactive, if it is not inactive.
    - You can use this follow-up action for example to reject an inspection lot based on visual characteristics, but the technical condition is still valid.
  • EAM_CL_CHG_TOBJ_STATUS_FLCO (Checklists Follow-Up Action: Set or reset FLCO on tech obj based on UD) 
    This follow-up action can be used to dynamically set or remove the system status FLCO for the technical object of an inspection lot based on a predefined logic that considers the inspection lot valuation: A Rejected usage decision sets the status FLCO on the technical object. An Accepted usage decision removes the status FLCO from the technical object.

Note that the system status FLCO is informational in nature and does not trigger any business transactions on its own. However, you can control the execution of specific business transactions as a consequence of this status by defining custom user statuses that react to the setting and resetting of this status.

Integrated, cross-app navigation from the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app and Find Maintenance Orders app through BAdI implementation

The new Business Add-In (BAdI) Cross-App Navigation Control for Fiori Applications (EAM_CROSS_APP_NAV_CONTROL) allows you to display custom cross-app navigation links in the following SAP Fiori apps:

  • Find Maintenance Orders (F2175)
  • Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders (F4604)

The custom cross-app navigation allows you to open selected items in other Fiori apps, including apps that are
not part of SAP S/4HANA.

When you create an implementation, the additional quick action Open In becomes available in the table toolbar of the app. Business users can use this quick action to display a dropdown menu with links to the target applications that you have defined in the implementation.
In your implementation, you can define relevant target applications, link texts, and system behavior of the
cross-app navigation according to your requirements. If a target application is not included in the business
catalog assignment of a user, the respective link will not be visible.

The BAdI is available for Key User Extensibility in the Custom Logic app for for business context:

  • Asset Management: Maintenance Order
  • Maintenance Notification

If you have set up the integration of SAP S/4HANA Asset Management with SAP Asset Performance

object and the failure mode. With this feature, you can get recommendations for the task list in the Create
Maintenance Request (F1511A) app.

The next figure shows the integration scenario:

  1. Initiate Work Request
  2. Technical Object and/or Failure Data
  3. Propose Task Lists based on implemented Recommendations & addressed Failure Data
  4. Re-/Assignment of Task List based on  APM recommended Task List or S/4HANA value help
  5. Optional usage of the processing context emergency work will create an order in the execution phase with the exploded assigned task list

If you request maintenance for a specific technical object in the Create Maintenance Request app, you can

The next screenshot shows the Create Maintenance Request app with the following steps:

  1. The Task List field will be enabled, if the user has the authorization for the Emergency Work processing context (Authorization check against object I_EXCP_MN with field name ACTVT = 03 (processing context))

By the way: The button was renamed from Recommended Task List to Get Recommended Task Lists in Screen Maintenance Requests (F4072) app.
  3. Displaying additional information to support an easier task list choice.

    - Technical Object

Providing a catalog-based “Failure Effect” field in the SAP Fiori apps for maintenance requests

Technicians can now record failure effects when processing maintenance requests in SAP Fiori. While the failure mode describes the way in which a technical object fails to perform a required function, the failure effect describes the evidence or the symptom that shows that a failure has occurred.
The failure effect is part of the failure data hierarchy in SAP Asset Performance Management and is now introduced in the apps for processing maintenance requests in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management. Several apps have now been enhanced with a new field, allowing you to capture this information in your maintenance
requests.
Technicians can report the failure effect while creating a maintenance request by selecting a suitable code from the catalog profile of the technical object or notification type. As a supervisor, you can change the failure effect after inspecting the situation on site. The specified failure effect is visible for maintenance technicians while performing the repair work.
This feature improves the quality of data that is recorded during failure detection and maintenance execution to facilitate reliable failure analytics and the development of maintenance strategies based on methodologies such as Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) or Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA). It also further aligns the failure data model in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management with technical standards such as IEC 60300 and IEC 60812.

Customizing of catalog-based Failure Effect: Catalog Profile and Catalog

The figure below shows the customizing steps of catalog-based failure effect:

  3. Used catalog for the failure effect field during maintenance processing

Enabling situation handling for maintenance task lists and maintenance plans 

The extended framework of Situation Handling supports you in creating custom situations. Two kinds of situations are supported: object-based and message-based situations.
The following figure gives you an overview of the process flow for custom object-based situations, including the apps and roles that are involved:

Figure 48: Process Flow for Custom Object-Based Situations

For more information, see Situation Handling - Extended Framework.

Situation Handling now delivers the following situation templates for Maintenance Management:

  • Maintenance Plan (EAM_MAINTENANCE_PLAN) - This situation template allows you to create situation
    types that can trigger situations when a maintenance plan is created or changed. From the situation
    instance details, you can navigate to the related app Manage Maintenance Plans to view details of the plan.
  • Maintenance Item (EAM_MAINTENANCE_ITEM) - This situation template allows you to create situation
    types that can trigger situations when a maintenance item is created, assigned, or changed. From the
    situation instance details, you can navigate to the related app Manage Maintenance Items to view details of the item.
  • Maintenance Task List (EAM_MAINT_TASK_LIST) - This situation template allows you to create situation
    types that can trigger situations when a maintenance task list is created or changed. From the situation
    instance details, you can navigate to the related app Find Maintenance Task List to view details of the task
    list.

Based on the situation templates, you can create situation types in the Manage Situation Types - Extended app. In a situation type, you can define the conditions and rules based on which situation instances are created to notify you when a situation occurs. Choose a situation instance to view details of the situation. You are taken to the My Situations - Extended app. You can choose the related app to view details of the business object.

The figure below shows an example use case in the Manage Situation Types - Extended app with the following parameters:

  • Situation object:     EAM_MAINT_ITEM
  • Situation scenario:  EAM_MAINTENANCE_ITEM_PROCESSING
  • Situation template:  EAM_MAINTENANCE_ITEM

Improve accuracy of material costs in refurbishment process

Business Context of Refurbishment Process related to Material Cost and Settlement

This feature enables you to make the system automatically settle an order of the order type refurbishment order (PM04) when it has been delivered to the warehouse. This is especially valuable if the materials have a moving average price.

In the settlement rule of the refurbishment order, only the inventory-managed material can be determined as the cost receiver. When the refurbishment is executed, actual costs incur as a result of consumption or confirmation postings. However, the moving average price of the material in the material master is recalculated not before the settlement of the refurbishment order. If the repairable spares have been refurbished and been delivered to the warehouse again but the refurbishment order has not yet been settled, the incorrect moving average price leads to incorrect planned and actual costs in maintenance orders that use this material as spare parts.

Improve accuracy of the moving average price of a material

With this feature, you can determine that the system automatically settles the refurbishment order each time when goods receipts have been posted for all units of the material. Only if all units of the material in the refurbishment order have been delivered to the warehouse, the Delivery Completed indicator is selected and the system status Delivered (DLV) is set on order header level. With the order settlement, the moving average price in the material master record is recalculated, the refurbished material in the warehouse is valued with the correct price, and as a result goods issues of the repairable spares are evaluated correctly. This leads to accurate planned and actual costs in the corresponding maintenance orders.

How to enable the automatic order settlement?

The following SAP Notes are required:

  • 3458343 - Business Event: Status of refurbishment order set to delivered
  • 3456964 - Automatic settlement of refurbishment orders

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

AI-assisted Maintenance Order Recommendation

​In the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app, you can now select a single notification and create a maintenance order using an existing order as a reference. Instead of selecting the reference order manually, you can let the system recommend suitable maintenance orders that have been created for similar issues in the past. The recommendations are generated using machine learning capabilities of SAP AI Services.

In the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app, the user interface for creating a new maintenance order has been enhanced. When you select a single maintenance notification and choose Create Order,

Figure 67: 'Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders' app - Create Order

the system displays a popup with a new Copy from Order field and a Get Recommendations button. ​

Figure 68: 'Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders' app - Create Order pop-up with active 'Get Recommendations' button

Once you have completed the required setup steps and created a trained machine learning model, this button is active and allows maintenance planners to retrieve recommendations for suitable reference orders that are generated by a machine learning algorithm.

​After you have pressed Get Recommendation the AI will propose the three best matching orders for your selected notification to copy from. You can expand this list up to six recommendations by pressing the More action.

Figure 69: 'Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders' app - Order Recommendations

Each recommended order is displayed as a card on the pop-up. The pop-up includes three pop-overs for Order, Technical Object, and Operations which should give the maintenance planner more details to the recommended orders. For instance, the next screenshot shows the pop-over for Operations:

Figure 70: 'Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders' app - recommended order with pop-over for operations details

​If one of the recommended orders fits as a reference order for you, select the card and press Select.

Please note that for this innovation the commercialisation is pending (status: November 2024). Please contact your SAP account manager for more details.

(🔙Table of Contents)

Mass change of custom fields using the “Manage Maintenance Plans” and “Manage Maintenance Items” apps

With the Manage Maintenance Plans (F5325) app and the Manage Maintenance Items (F5356) app, you can now change custom fields of multiple maintenance plans and maintenance items respectively by choosing the Mass Change button. Previously, you were only able to perform mass changes on predefined fields. But now, you can also perform mass changes on custom fields that you have created. This flexibility allows you to tailor the system to your specific needs and streamline your maintenance processes.

First, we delivered this innovation with the 2408 release in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, see 2408 Blog Post. In the video below I show you the mass change of custom fields in the Manage Maintenance Plans app:

Video 1: Mass Change Custom Fields of Maintenance Plans

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

Mass Generation of Permit to Work Objects

With this feature, you can automatically generate work permits and any associated safety certificates for
maintenance orders using the program Mass Generation of Permit to Work Objects (transaction PTWMASSGEN).

The work permits are generated with reference to a work permit template and assigned to the respective
maintenance orders.
You can use various selection criteria to define the set of orders for which you would like to generate work
permits. This includes the possibility to generate additional work permits for orders with existing work permit
assignments. Furthermore, you can either specify a static work permit template or determine that the system
should select the appropriate work permit templates for each maintenance order automatically based on
the determination logic you have defined and the template assignments you have maintained for matching
combinations of order type, planning plant, technical object, and task list. The program also offers a test run
option to perform the relevant validations without creating any work permits.

Figure 71: Program 'Mass Generation of Permit to Work Objects' (transaction PTWMASSGEN)

Implementation Details

To use the automatic template determination, you need to perform the following steps:

  1. Define the template determination logic by implementing the new Business Add-In
    BADI_PTW_DTMN_TEMPLATES (BAdI: Template Determination for Generation of Work Permits). You can find this BAdI in Customizing for Plant Maintenance and Customer Service under Work Clearance
    Management Permit to Work Enhancements. SAP provides an example implementation with a
    predefined determination logic. The BAdI allows you to create different implementations depending on
    the planning plant.
  2. Assign work permit templates to specific combinations of planning plant, technical object, task list, and
    order type using the mapping table in transaction PTWDTMNTMPL.

Customizing
In order to use the mass generation of permit to work objects, the following settings are required in the relevant Customizing activities under Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Work Clearance Management -> Permit to Work :

  • You have defined internal number ranges for work permits and safety certificates.
  • You have defined a reference work permit type for the relevant work permit templates and a reference
    safety certificate type for the relevant safety certificate templates.

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

More Information / Additional Links

Thanks for reading this blog post. Stay tuned!

Check out also my previous blog posts about Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition:

  • Expert Talk: Inspection Checklist in Maintenance Management – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • Expert Talk: Getting Started with Phase Model in Maintenance Management
  • Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | 2023 FPS01 Release
  • Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023
  • Highlights for Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition and SAP S/4HANA | 2022 Release
  • Highlights for Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA 2021

If you want to learn more about SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, join our Partner Community. This group focuses on providing enhanced learnings and periodic updates concerning the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition product. Our goal is to facilitate collaboration with your peers and SAP subject matter experts.

For more information on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, check out the following links:

  • The Link Collection for Asset Management
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition release info: sap.com/s4hana
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Community here
  • SAP S/4HANA PSCC Digital Enablement Wheel here
  • Inside SAP S/4HANA Podcast here
  • Best practices for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition here
  • Help Portal Product Page here
  • Feature Scope Description here
  • What’s New here

Follow the SAP S/4HANA Cloud tag and the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA, or myself via @GerhardWelker and LinkedIn

(🔙Table of Contents)

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2411
entry.content:
This month's bumper update is filled with exciting innovations to elevate your asset performance management. We'll start this edition with the latest highlights for asset strategy, then move on to the exciting first release of industrial systems, providing you with multiple perspectives on technical systems. We'll then introduce you to our new asset health features with embedded IoT and visual condition monitoring capabilities using images and AI. Let's dive in!

 

Asset Risk Assessment

Mark an impact as relevant or not relevant in a Risk and Criticality Assessment

Effective asset management requires precise and adaptable evaluation of risks and their impacts. The ability to exclude non-essential impacts in Risk and Criticality assessments provides organizations with the flexibility to tailor assessments to specific operational needs. Whether managing assets across diverse locations or varying technical objects, this functionality ensures assessments focus solely on what truly matters, improving accuracy and decision-making.

Key Benefits of Impact Exclusion

  • Tailored Assessments: By excluding irrelevant impacts, you can create focused evaluations that align with specific business contexts, such as regional differences or unique technical object requirements.
  • Improved Clarity: Marked exclusions are clearly labeled within the assessment, helping teams concentrate on the most critical factors and document reasons for exclusions.
  • Accurate Risk Scores: Excluded impacts are automatically omitted from calculations, ensuring that the overall risk score reflects only the relevant high-risk elements without distortion.

This feature empowers businesses to:

  • Streamline Assessments: Focus only on essential impacts to reduce noise and enhance decision-making.
  • Ensure Consistency: Apply exclusions uniformly across similar objects or scenarios, improving the reliability of risk scores.
  • Save Time and Effort: Use templates and Excel integration to efficiently manage assessments at scale.

By enabling teams to concentrate on critical factors and eliminate unnecessary data, businesses can enhance their risk evaluations and achieve more actionable insights. Watch the video to find out more.

 

Asset Reliability Engineering

Mark a strategy assessment for classes as a 'template'

Consistency, control, and traceability are essential for effective strategy implementation in asset management. Marking a strategy assessment for classes as a template helps standardize processes, improve governance, and simplify reassessment efforts. Templates serve as a central reference, allowing organizations to align strategy assessments with operational goals while maintaining flexibility and oversight.

Key Business Benefits of Using Templates

  • Central Governance: Templates provide a consistent framework for assessments, reducing variability and supporting standardization.
  • Improved Traceability: The ability to track copies of Templates and linked assessments makes it easier to manage updates and maintain alignment.
  • Simplified Processes: Templates streamline the creation and replication of assessments, saving time and ensuring uniformity.

How Templates Support Governance

Templates are designed to be used as references rather than directly implemented, ensuring better control over assessments:

  • No Technical Object Assignments: Templates cannot link to technical objects, preserving their role as reusable standards.
  • Controlled Recommendations: Recommendations associated with Templates are always in a "Released" status to maintain consistency.

Visibility into Template usage is another key feature:

  • Copy Tracking: Filters and the Template Copy Count feature allow users to monitor how many assessments are derived from a Template, making it easier to apply updates when needed.

Business Impact

Using Templates for strategy assessments allows organizations to:

  • Maintain Consistency: Ensure assessments follow a standard approach without additional effort.
  • Simplify Updates: Quickly identify and revise assessments that are based on a Template, ensuring changes are applied consistently.
  • Save Time: Replicate standardized assessments efficiently, speeding up processes while maintaining quality.

Watch the video to see it in action:

 

Reset recommendation through authorization control

Efficient asset management requires the ability to adapt recommendations as needs evolve. Now, authorized users can reset statuses like 'Implemented' to 'Implementation in Process' or 'Released' to 'In Process', enabling updates to technical objects, maintenance plans, and more.

Key Benefits

  • Flexibility: Adjust recommendations to align with changing requirements.
  • Governance: Role-based controls ensure secure, authorized updates.
  • Transparency: Comments for status changes enhance traceability.

By streamlining rework processes and enabling updates under strict controls, this feature enhances agility and accuracy in recommendation management.

 

Asset Information and Data Management

Manage Industrial Systems

Efficiently managing complex systems is a critical challenge in asset-heavy industries like Oil & Gas and Railways. To address this, we have a new application Manage Industrial Systems, a powerful master data concept designed to simplify and enhance asset management by providing multiple perspectives on technical systems.

What Are Industrial Systems?

Based on the ISO 81346 standard, an Industrial System is a collection of interrelated objects defined within a specific context. It provides three distinct views, or aspects, of technical systems:

  • Functional View: Focused on operational roles and processes.
  • Product-Related View: Centered on the components and equipment.
  • Location-Based View: Highlighting physical and geographical contexts.

How It Works

Using the Manage Industrial Systems application in SAP Asset Performance Management, users can create and model system structures, linking them seamlessly to technical objects such as functional locations and equipment in SAP ERP. This integration ensures that Industrial Systems are fully aligned with existing asset structures, eliminating redundancy and enhancing accessibility.

Business Benefits

  • Enhanced Flexibility: Move beyond a single leading structure to view and manage assets from multiple perspectives.
  • Operational Efficiency: Simplify navigation and access to assets, reducing time spent on asset identification and management.
  • Seamless Integration: Align technical systems across SAP ERP and Asset Performance Management, ensuring consistency and continuity in asset data.
  • Improved Decision-Making: Access to varied perspectives allows for better insights and strategic planning.
  • With Industrial Systems, SAP Asset Performance Management empowers industries to tackle the complexity of asset management with a structured, adaptable approach, driving operational efficiency and better asset utilization.

Watch the video for a quick introduction to Industrial Systems:

 

Manage Reference Designator Libraries: The Foundation for Efficient Industrial Systems

As an administrator, creating a clear Reference Designator Scheme is essential for managing industrial systems effectively. This scheme uses object class codes to unambiguously identify each system aspect, serving as the foundation for building the system hierarchy. By assigning unique Reference Designator codes to each system element, you ensure clarity and structure, making it easier to organize and manage complex assets across the system.

Prerequisites in the Configuration Manager

Before using the Manage Reference Designator Libraries application, certain configurations need to be completed in the Configuration Manager application:

  • Industry Domains: Define the areas where your industrial systems operate (e.g., Oil & Gas, Railways). While predefined domains are available, you can create custom domains as needed.
  • Reference Designator Sources: Set the reference designator standards, such as ISO, DIN, IEEE, or ANSI, that will be used to structure the system.
  • Aspects: Specify the different ways to structure your industrial systems (e.g., Function, Product, or Location), allowing you to filter system elements based on these categories.
  • Relationship Types: Define how system elements are related to one another (e.g., "realized" or "located"). These relationships help organize the system hierarchy.

Once these configurations are complete, you are ready to use the Manage Reference Designator Libraries application to build and maintain your system structure.

Key features of the Manage Reference Designator Libraries application

  • Set Up Reference Designators: Using the Reference Designator Scheme, administrators can create a system hierarchy where each element is identified using a unique Reference Designator code. This ensures clarity, consistency, and ease of management.
  • Create and Maintain Hierarchies: With the Reference Designator Library, you can build and manage system hierarchies by linking system elements and their relationships. This organization ensures that assets are logically structured and easily navigable.
  • Import and Export Data: The app supports importing and exporting object classes via Excel. This feature makes it simple to update, add, or modify system elements without manually entering data, saving time and reducing errors.

Business Benefits

  • Improved Organizational Clarity: With a clear system hierarchy based on structured Reference Designators, your team can quickly identify and manage assets, reducing confusion and errors.
  • Increased Flexibility: The ability to customize domains, standards, and aspects gives you the flexibility to tailor the system to your specific industry needs, ensuring your asset management system adapts to changing requirements.
  • Enhanced Efficiency: Streamlined data import and export processes ensure quick updates to the system, while pre-configured elements save time during system setup and maintenance.
  • Global Compliance: By adhering to international standards, you ensure that your system is compliant with global regulations, helping maintain consistency and reliability across multiple regions.

The Manage Reference Designator Libraries application is essential for building and managing efficient industrial systems. By providing a structured approach to identifying and organizing system elements, it enhances clarity, flexibility, and operational efficiency, while ensuring compliance with global standards. Watch this video to see how it works:

 

Asset Health Monitoring

Visual Condition Monitoring Using Images and AI

Effective asset management is critical to minimizing downtime and ensuring operational efficiency. With visual condition monitoring using images and AI, you can proactively identify potential issues and take timely action. With our current beta release, you can:

  • Configure visual inspection indicators
  • View visual inspection results in the indicator monitoring chart via Explore Technical Objects
  • Configure alert types, data sets and rules
  • Process alerts from the alert lists using supporting visual images providing feedback to improve AI-generated results.

Watch this space for an upcoming dedicated blog on visual condition monitoring using images and AI but here is a quick introduction.

Configure visual inspection indicators

To do visual condition monitoring using images and AI, define the indicator as a "visual inspection indicator". A picture icon appears on the indicator, making it easy to differentiate visual inspection indicators from other metrics.

View visual inspection results in the Indicator Monitoring chart via Explore Technical Objects

The system displays measurements and images in the indicator monitoring chart with visualized minimum and maximum thresholds. If a measurement exceeds the threshold, an alert is triggered, enabling early issue detection.

Configure alert types, data sets and rules

Once alert types, data sets and rules are set up, the asset conditions are evaluated based on the defined threshold and schedule.

Process alerts from the alert lists using supporting visual images

If a critical condition, like low thickness, is met, an alert is generated, ensuring timely attention and minimizing maintenance delays. The alert contains an image of the part in question, supporting reliability engineers in deciding on the next maintenance steps.

By combining visual condition monitoring using images with automated alerts, you can streamline your condition monitoring process, improve asset management efficiency, and proactively address maintenance needs. Watch the video to see the end-to-end process.

 

Condition-Based Monitoring with Alerts and Embedded IoT

We're introducing new and improved functionalities for users of Embedded IoT tenants. Watch this space for a dedicated blog but here's a quick introduction to the new features.

Manage Data Sets Application

This application allows users to define data sets, which can then be consumed by the configured rules. Data sets act as the basis for defining aggregations and their respective time windows, enabling their use both during rule design and at runtime. The same data set can be reused for monitoring and derivation rules.

New Rules Design-Time in Embedded IoT Tenants

One of the most significant updates in this release is the introduction of a new rules design-time for Embedded IoT tenants. This includes:

  • Monitoring rules - to monitor the condition of a technical object.
  • Derivation rules - to enable the calculation of indicator values, including their timestamps, by deriving them from other indicator values based on specified conditions.

Scheduling Flexibility

The new rules can be configured to run based on a defined schedule. This flexibility ensures that rules are executed at optimal times, enhancing performance and efficiency. Currently, the most frequent scheduling interval supported is every 5 minutes.

Dynamic Indicator Chart

There's a new indicator chart which visualizes values across time series ranges with the minimum, maximum, and average values. This is useful when dealing with datasets that have significant fluctuations or disparities.

Simplified Data Integration

Embedded IoT introduces a streamlined Data Integration channel, ideal if you have extensive data lakes or batch data requirements. This feature supports large data transfers with pre-configured intervals (e.g., daily or hourly) and structured file ingestion, simplifying the management of time series data. This is perfect if you are relying on big data analytics, as it enables seamless handling of large datasets without the need for real-time processing.

The file upload interface allows for the efficient, compressed transfer of time series data, this documentation details the steps to set up a process to ingest compressed files. Users can easily access the necessary information for preparing data packages and monitoring processing status via APM's API interfaces. With Embedded IoT, assets are automatically enabled to retrieve time series data, eliminating the need for manual 'IoT Sync'. This simplifies connectivity to time series data sources, enhancing business value by optimizing the data integration process.

Subscription Updates: Important Details for Users

If you have an existing subscription to SAP Asset Performance Management with the material code 8012342, there are some essential updates to note.

Under the current material code, you can only use the Embedded IoT (EIoT) tenants for testing purposes.

Switching to Productive Usage

To transition to using EIoT tenants for productive purposes, please get in touch with your SAP account team. They will assist you in switching to the latest material, ensuring you have the proper setup for your business needs.

Further Information

For additional details, please refer to SAP Note 3523368. This document provides comprehensive information on the changes and how to proceed.

Watch this video to see a step-by-step process:

Keep In Touch

Here's a quick reminder how to keep in touch with all things APM:

  • Check out all past and present Intelligent Asset Management webcasts here. If you register for an IAM webcast, you will be automatically subscribed to our IAM Community Engagement Newsletter. 
  • Check some of our recent blogs:
    • Why Failure-Mode Focus Matters in Intelligent Asset Management 
    • SAP Asset Performance Management Embedding Cumulocity IoT to Drive Innovations in IoT and AI
    • How AI-based Anomaly Detection empowers reliability engineers in SAP Asset Performance Management
  • Subscribe to What's New on the SAP Help Portal
  • Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session

See you again soon for the 2412 release update!

entry.title: Feedback from the water & wastewater leadership day 2024, Brussels december 5th
entry.content:
Every year, it is becoming a tradition to meet a significant number of water and wastewater services companies interested on innovative strategies and technologies to enhance water resilience and drive digital transformation in their companies. Following the tradition established with the SAP Executive Value Network for Water and Wastewater companies meetings held in Europe and Latin America over the past few years, this year we met in Brussels delegates from 16 companies together with software and system integrators including ESRI, Capgemini, digit’Eaux and SAP.  

 

 

 

The agenda focused on four main topics:  The importance of sector collaboration for innovation, developing a corporate AI strategy, Smart Metering/Smart Water platform, and the adoption of spatial technologies, with inspiring presentations to facilitate comprehensive debates and discussions as related in the following agenda:

  • The importance of sector collaboration:  Flanders collaborative experience for innovation in the Water Sector:  strategy and benefits by De Watergroep
  • Developing an AI strategy in a public company by FARYS
  • Smart Water Platform by Capgemini:
    • Use cases from business perspective
    • Leveraging data intelligence beyond smart metering: Water Balance, energy management and AI driven cases
  • Test and analytics of digital water meters by Suez
  • ESRI on HANA DB:  advantages and possibilities combining data from S/4HANA by ESRI
  • Leveraging spatial data to build strategic asset management by SAP

SAP Keynote by Alain Maus and Miquel Carbó

The first keynote and subject for discussion has been provided by Bert de Winter from De Watergroep who shared his company's experience of collaboration for innovation in the Water Sector among the water companies in the Flemish region, describing the strategy and benefits obtained. It was a very well received presentation, as this approach has proven to deliver significant benefits, such as economies of scale, which cannot easily be achieved by individual approaches. It is also the foundation of the Smart Water Platform, an ongoing collaborative project among De Watergroep in cooperation with FARYS and PIDPA water companies to achieve business outcomes identified around the usage of smart meter data.

Mrs Inge Opreel, CIO of FARYS changed the dynamic of the group discussion by presenting the concepts and steps taken to define a strategy for the adoption, integration, and responsible use of AI in her company.  This presentation generated lots of interest and questions among peer attendees, as they are currently facing the need to evolve from “POCs” – Proof of Concept deliverables – into a much more consistent policy to leverage Artificial Intelligence offerings to improve and achieve gains in operational efficiency, customer engagement, sustainability and other key areas of the company. The discussion also included a discussion of the many challenges associated with AI as well as the need for clarification and common understanding of the term AI and what areas it covers as well as how to explain it. Regarding these mentioned points, a very interesting suggestion by FARYs is to create a “Decision Framework” where to evaluate for each potential use case the adoption criteria can be evaluated based on difference between local and internet connected AI/LLM’s as well as decisions on Sensitivity/privacy of data, ownership of data, need for actual information, need for adaptation, latency, reliability and TCO.

Kevin De Wilde, Engagement Manager and Thought Leader at Capgemini presented a series of use cases of the Smart Water Platform to showcase the value provided by this implementation with the aim of digitizing water networks to address sustainability challenges. After an introductory view of its functional architecture and characteristics, presented some cases of anomalies detected among the installed base of smart meters:  continuous consumption, Backflow / no check valve and complex reports and analysis like the Network water remote quality monitoring and the Network water flow monitoring (water balance, leak detection, ILI, …)

Inge Opreel (FARYS), Bert de Winter (De Watergroep) Kevin de Wilde (Capgemini)

Roseline Klein, from SUEZ Digital Solutions introduced the audience to the Technical Centre for Metering and Measurement (CTCM - Centre Technique Comptage et Mesure) which is responsible for defining the rules for managing the metering and measuring instruments used in drinking water and wastewater in SUEZ operations, but which is also increasingly providing expert support to all players in the metering and instrumentation sector, both in France and abroad, with regards to any problems they may encounter.  Since its creation in 1999, the CTCM works closely with numerous external parties to support R&D efforts and improvement of metering resulting >5.00 meters and instruments controlled each year.  Metering performance and analysis:  regulatory controls and EMP (Weighted Average Error), feedback consolidation and anomaly detection, dispute handling, monitoring and understanding of consumption trends and control and monitoring of strategic meters.

The spatial part of the discussion was inspired by the outstanding presentation of Jeroen van Winden from ESRI. He talked about the multiple levels of integration between ArcGIS and SAP including HANA DB, including some interesting use cases and examples, showing as well cases of integration using API's on web services and at application level in both platforms. This slot was complemented by Miquel Carbó from SAP addressing in his presentation how to leverage spatial data to build strategic asset management, this is a wholistic view of the entire lifecycle of the technical assets, both from the execution part (Investment to Comission or Inspections and corrective maintenance) or in the proactive part (asset risk analysis, health indexes or asset investment planning scenarios) where in all these capabilities the spatial dimension is fundamental.

Jeroen Van Winden (ESRI), Roseline Klein (Suez Digital Solutions), Miquel Carbó (SAP)

Overall, the session was intense and the networking opportunies were very high. The collaboration among SAP and the participant companies has once again raised the expectations of the delegates from the Water and Waste Water industry by providing such high quality inspiring speeches and facilitating fluent and interesting networking to a level that makes it very difficult but a motivating challenge to keep the level for next year!

entry.title: SAP Utilities Roadmap 2025 Onwards
entry.content:
The utilities industry will continue to play a key role in SAP's strategy and development topics. It is therefore important to us to provide customers, partners, and the entire SAP Utilities ecosystem with information about the planned innovations.

This year we have once again created a matrix of the respective industry topics and the corresponding innovations. This matrix represents an excerpt of the most important innovations and does not claim to be complete. Over time, further innovations may be added, and delivery periods may also change.

After an overview of the highlights, we will list the details for each topic; behind almost all innovations you will find a link to the SAP Roadmap Explorer with further details.

 

SAP Asset Performance Management

  • AI-powered Joule digital assistant for navigational and transactional use cases Link
  • AI-enabled visual inspection for asset condition monitoring (general availability) Link
  • Advanced asset health monitoring scenarios with embedded IoT Link
  • Custom AI models and algorithms Link

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

  • Utilizing artificial intelligence to streamline creation and planning of maintenance orders Link
  • Enable external collaboration with subcontractors and service providers Link
  • Enabling integration of service with advanced execution orders (billable maintenance) Link
  • Supporting linear fields in Find Maintenance Order and Operation Link (and many more roadmap items supporting linear assets)
  • Isolation management in a permit to work (lockout tagout) Link

SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration

  • Mass import export APIs for main technical objects Link
  • Digital nameplate Link
  • Enablement of digital twin handover with procurement Link

SAP Field Service Management

  • AI-assisted browsing of knowledge bases Link
  • Multiple technicians in auto-scheduling – crews Link
  • Fully automated planning on the service map Link

SAP Service and Asset Manager

  • Field service crew support Link
  • Management of non-stock items Link
